refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution's design permits completion of load→travel→dump within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across representative worksite trials; if p95 ≤ CT_target, the FR is satisfied. If p95 > CT_target, investigate whether the failure is due to (a) inadequate DP (design cannot support the target) or (b) operational/environmental factors outside the DP scope.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the load phase (bucket fill and engagement) to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the travel phase (loaded and unloaded transit) to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the dump phase (material discharge and bucket retraction) to complete within the allocated time budget.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time controller that monitors elapsed time against phase budgets and dynamically adjusts operational parameters (speed, acceleration, dwell) to meet p95 target without exceeding it.

DP3: Design modular subsystem interfaces (propulsion, hydraulics, control) such that each subsystem exposes cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (e.g., load-phase duration, travel speed capability) enabling predictable composition.

DP4: Establish a duty-cycle characterization model (payload mass distribution, terrain profile, ambient conditions) and use it to pre-compute phase-time budgets offline; execute cycle with phase-time enforcement.

DP5: Implement adaptive cycle execution with real-time feedback loops on phase completion times, comparing p95 rolling statistics against target and triggering parameter tuning (within safety/operational constraints) to converge toward target.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3 + DP4 (integrated): Design modular subsystem interfaces that expose cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (load duration, travel speed, dump duration) and establish a duty-cycle characterization model to pre-compute phase-time budgets offline. Execute cycle with phase-time enforcement via phase-gating logic (DP1 foundation). This approach balances predictability (offline budgeting) with modularity (independent subsystem optimization) and avoids tight coupling between control logic and subsystem internals.

potential_sources: Risk: If subsystem interfaces do not cleanly expose phase-relevant metrics, control logic may become tightly coupled to subsystem implementation details (e.g., hydraulic pressure curves, motor speed tables). Mitigation: Define a canonical set of phase-performance contracts (e.g., 'load_phase_duration(payload_mass, site_condition) → time_estimate') that subsystems must implement.

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization model (DP4) may not capture all site variability (e.g., unexpected terrain, operator behavior). Mitigation: Include model-update mechanisms and fallback to conservative budgets if real-time p95 exceeds target.

Risk: Phase-gating logic (DP1) may introduce artificial sequencing delays if phases are not truly independent. Mitigation: Validate that load, travel, and dump phases have minimal overlap or dependency; if overlap is necessary, revise phase definitions.

Risk: Offline budget pre-computation assumes stable duty cycle; high variability may require online re-budgeting. Mitigation: Combine DP4 with lightweight DP5 feedback for adaptive tuning within operational constraints.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids naming specific actuators, sensors, or control algorithms. It establishes logical boundaries (subsystem interfaces, phase contracts, duty-cycle model) that allow independent evolution of propulsion, hydraulics, and control without requiring redesign of the cycle-time architecture. Modularity is preserved by requiring subsystems to expose performance metrics rather than internal state.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Cycle phases (load, travel, dump) can be cleanly sequenced with minimal overlap. Risk: If operational constraints (e.g., safety interlocks, terrain) force phases to overlap, phase-gating logic may not be applicable. Mitigation: Validate phase independence during requirements refinement.

Assumption: Duty-cycle characterization model can be built with sufficient fidelity from historical data or simulation. Risk: Model may be inaccurate for novel site conditions or payload distributions. Mitigation: Include model validation gates and fallback to conservative budgets.

Assumption: Subsystems can reliably estimate phase durations given input parameters (payload, terrain, ambient). Risk: Subsystem estimates may be optimistic or subject to wear/degradation. Mitigation: Include margin in budget allocation and real-time monitoring.

Assumption: p95 cycle time is the appropriate acceptance criterion. Risk: If p95 is driven by rare outliers (e.g., single stuck valve), optimizing p95 may not improve typical performance. Mitigation: Validate that p95 is representative of operational variability; consider median + percentile bands.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose hard constraints that conflict with cycle-time optimization. Risk: Safety interlocks or operator procedures may add unavoidable delays. Mitigation: Incorporate safety/operational constraints into phase-budget allocation from the start.

Assumption: Cycle-time target is achievable with the selected propulsion and hydraulic subsystems. Risk: Target may be infeasible without subsystem redesign. Mitigation: Validate target against subsystem performance curves early; revise target or subsystems if necessary.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall execute the load→travel→dump cycle such that the p95 measured cycle time, under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, does not exceed CT_target, while maintaining compliance with worksite safety plan and operational policies.' This clarifies that cycle-time optimization is constrained by safety and operational boundaries. Additionally, define 'duty cycle' formally (e.g., payload mass range, terrain profile, ambient temperature range, operator skill level) to enable DP4 (duty-cycle characterization model) to be scoped and validated. Finally, establish a phase-performance contract template (e.g., 'subsystem X shall provide load_phase_duration(payload_mass, site_condition) with ±X% accuracy') to support DP3 (modular subsystem interfaces) and enable independent subsystem verification.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated duty-cycle characterization model, phase-time budget pre-computation engine, cycle-phase performance metric exposure layer, and phase-gating enforcement logic. Encapsulates the offline budgeting subsystem and real-time phase-time enforcement mechanism that coordinates with Vehicle subsystem via standardized interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution exports cycle-phase performance metrics (load duration, travel speed, dump duration) and phase-time budget status to external monitoring/logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives cycle task definitions and parameters from task scheduler; uses these to trigger offline duty-cycle characterization and phase-time budget pre-computation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits phase-gating enforcement signals and cycle-phase constraint violations to Safety Services for real-time safety arbitration.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution may receive subsystem performance telemetry from Vehicle (e.g., actual load, speed feedback) to refine phase-time budgets and enforce phase gates; sends phase-time enforcement commands back to Vehicle control subsystems.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the natural home for duty-cycle characterization, offline budget pre-computation, and phase-gating logic. These are core algorithmic and control-flow responsibilities that belong in the guidance/execution layer. Minimal diffusion: only exports metrics via Telemetry and receives task parameters via Tasking; phase-gate enforcement is internal state machine. Vehicle subsystem remains decoupled from budget logic.

Poor containment. Vehicle is a hardware abstraction layer for actuators and sensors. Placing duty-cycle characterization and phase-time budgeting here would couple low-level vehicle control to high-level cycle planning logic. Phase-gating would require Vehicle to understand task-level semantics (load duration, dump duration), violating separation of concerns. Significant diffusion: NGHE Solution would need to reach into Vehicle internals to enforce phase gates, and Vehicle would need to expose cycle-phase awareness.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface layer for remote operation. Duty-cycle characterization, offline budgeting, and phase-gating are autonomous execution concerns, not teleoperation concerns. Placing DP3+DP4 here would create severe coupling between operator input and cycle-phase enforcement, and would require Teleoperation to maintain state about cycle phases and time budgets. High diffusion: logic would leak into NGHE Solution and Vehicle.

Poor containment. Safety Services is a constraint-enforcement and arbitration layer. While phase-gating has safety implications, the core DP (duty-cycle characterization, offline budgeting, phase-time enforcement) is a performance optimization and predictability mechanism, not a safety constraint. Placing it here would conflate safety logic with cycle optimization. High diffusion: NGHE Solution would still need to implement the budgeting model and phase-gating, and Vehicle would need to understand phase semantics.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate boundary for DP3+DP4 because it is the designated layer for autonomous guidance, execution planning, and real-time cycle control. The duty-cycle characterization model and phase-time budgeting are planning-domain responsibilities that feed into execution. Phase-gating logic is a real-time enforcement mechanism that operates at the guidance level, not at the vehicle hardware level. By localizing these concerns in NGHE Solution, we maintain clean separation: Vehicle remains a stateless actuator/sensor abstraction, Teleoperation remains a human-input layer, and Safety Services remains a constraint arbiter. The DP is cohesive within NGHE Solution because all three elements (characterization, budgeting, phase-gating) are tightly coupled in the execution flow and share state (phase timers, budget allocations).
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a monolithic 'god module' if additional cycle-control DPs are assigned to it without further decomposition. Mitigation: Establish internal sub-module boundaries within NGHE Solution (e.g., DutyCycleModel, PhaseGateController, BudgetExecutor) to maintain internal cohesion and testability.

Risk: Vehicle subsystem may lack sufficient telemetry fidelity (actual load, speed feedback) to allow NGHE Solution to refine phase-time budgets in real time. Mitigation: Define explicit Telemetry and Network Link contracts that specify which performance metrics Vehicle must expose; design NGHE Solution to gracefully degrade to pre-computed budgets if live feedback is unavailable.

Risk: Phase-gating enforcement may conflict with Safety Services constraints (e.g., emergency stop overrides phase gates). Mitigation: Establish clear priority rules: Safety Services constraints always override phase-gating; NGHE Solution must listen to SafetyAlert signals and abort or pause phase-gating as needed.

Risk: Offline duty-cycle characterization may become stale if Vehicle hardware or environmental conditions change significantly. Mitigation: Design NGHE Solution to periodically re-characterize duty cycles based on accumulated telemetry; expose re-characterization triggers via Tasking interface.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3+DP4 (integrated duty-cycle characterization, offline budget pre-computation, and phase-gating enforcement) directly addresses the FR requirement to enable execution of load→travel→dump cycle within customer-defined cycle time target. The DP provides a logically sound solution: offline budgeting ensures predictability of phase durations, phase-gating enforces adherence to budgets in real time, and modular subsystem interfaces allow independent optimization while maintaining cycle-time guarantees. The approach is verifiable against the acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target) by instrumenting phase-time enforcement and collecting telemetry.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Phase-gating logic may diffuse into Vehicle subsystem if real-time enforcement requires Vehicle to understand cycle-phase semantics (load vs. travel vs. dump). Mitigation: Enforce strict interface contract—Vehicle receives only phase-time enforcement commands (e.g., 'enforce 45s travel phase'), not cycle-phase definitions. NGHE Solution retains all phase-semantics logic.

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization model may diffuse across multiple modules if subsystem performance metrics (load duration, travel speed, dump duration) are not consistently exposed via standardized Telemetry interface. Mitigation: Define explicit IM boundary for metric exposure layer; all subsystems must publish metrics to a centralized telemetry bus that NGHE Solution consumes.

Risk: Offline budget pre-computation engine may become tightly coupled to specific Vehicle hardware if characterization model hard-codes subsystem-specific parameters. Mitigation: Parameterize the characterization model; accept subsystem performance profiles as inputs from Tasking interface, allowing re-characterization without code changes.

Risk: Phase-gating enforcement state (current phase, elapsed time, budget remaining) may leak into Safety Services if safety arbitration requires understanding of phase-time constraints. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface should be unidirectional (NGHE Solution → Safety Services); Safety Services overrides phase-gating via constraint signals, not by querying phase state.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The module boundary is well-defined: duty-cycle characterization, offline budgeting, and phase-gating are cohesive responsibilities that belong in the autonomous guidance/execution layer. Vehicle subsystem remains a stateless actuator/sensor abstraction (0.35 containment, 0.65 diffusion if DP were placed there—correctly rejected). Teleoperation and Safety Services are appropriately decoupled. Internal sub-module decomposition (DutyCycleModel, PhaseGateController, BudgetExecutor) is recommended to prevent NGHE Solution from becoming monolithic as additional cycle-control DPs are added. The IM boundary correctly encapsulates the offline subsystem and real-time enforcement mechanism; external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-scoped and minimize coupling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The IM boundary enables direct verification of the FR against acceptance criteria. NGHE Solution can instrument phase-time enforcement and export cycle-phase performance metrics (load duration, travel speed, dump duration) via Telemetry interface. Measured p95 cycle time can be collected and compared against CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Phase-gating enforcement signals can be logged to validate that cycle phases are executed within budgeted time windows. Real-time feedback from Vehicle (actual load, speed) via Network Link allows NGHE Solution to refine budgets and detect deviations.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the primary owner of cycle-time predictability and enforcement. Vehicle subsystem is allocated the responsibility to expose performance metrics and accept phase-time enforcement commands, but retains no cycle-planning logic. Tasking interface allocates the responsibility to provide cycle task definitions and parameters; NGHE Solution uses these to trigger offline characterization. Safety Services is allocated the responsibility to arbitrate phase-gating against safety constraints via SafetyAlert interface. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform the duty-cycle characterization model (e.g., maximum load weight, terrain conditions) but are not owned by any single module—they are inputs to the system design.

recommended_revisions: Establish internal sub-module boundaries within NGHE Solution: (1) DutyCycleCharacterizationModel—owns the offline budgeting algorithm and parameterization; (2) PhaseGateController—owns real-time phase-time enforcement state machine and gating logic; (3) TelemetryExposureLayer—owns metric aggregation and export via Telemetry interface. This prevents NGHE Solution from becoming a monolithic 'god module' and improves testability and maintainability.

Define explicit Telemetry contract specifying which performance metrics Vehicle must expose (load duration, travel speed, dump duration, actual vs. budgeted times) and at what frequency. Establish fallback behavior if Vehicle telemetry is unavailable or delayed (e.g., NGHE Solution degrades to pre-computed budgets).

Clarify SafetyAlert interface semantics: (1) NGHE Solution emits phase-gating enforcement signals and cycle-phase constraint violations to Safety Services; (2) Safety Services responds with override signals (e.g., 'abort phase-gating') that NGHE Solution must honor immediately. Define priority rules: Safety Services constraints always override phase-gating.

Design duty-cycle characterization model to accept subsystem performance profiles as parameterized inputs (not hard-coded). Enable periodic re-characterization triggered via Tasking interface based on accumulated telemetry, allowing adaptation to hardware wear or environmental changes.

Document the Network Link interface contract: specify which subsystem performance telemetry Vehicle sends to NGHE Solution (actual load, speed feedback) and which phase-time enforcement commands NGHE Solution sends to Vehicle. Ensure Vehicle does not need to understand cycle-phase semantics—only enforce time windows.

Add acceptance criterion for phase-gating enforcement: verify that actual phase durations remain within ±ε of budgeted durations (where ε is a tolerance derived from worksite safety plan). This ensures that phase-gating logic is functioning correctly and that offline budgets are realistic.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP (integrated duty-cycle characterization, offline budgeting, phase-gating enforcement) is logically sound, the IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is well-contained with 0.92 containment score and clear interface contracts, and verification is feasible via telemetry instrumentation. However, the recommended_revisions from the DP-IM gate explicitly call for internal sub-module decomposition within NGHE Solution to prevent monolithic design and improve testability. Given high safety criticality, decomposing the phase-gating enforcement logic, duty-cycle characterization model, and telemetry exposure layer into distinct sub-FRs is justified to ensure each sub-module can be independently verified and to clarify ownership of safety-critical phase-time enforcement. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will operationalize the architectural recommendations and enable detailed design of safety-critical interfaces.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Characterize duty-cycle phase durations (load, travel, dump) offline based on subsystem performance profiles and worksite parameters, producing a parameterized budget model that can be re-computed without code changes.

FR3.2: Enforce real-time phase-time gating such that each cycle phase (load, travel, dump) executes within its budgeted time window, with immediate abort capability triggered by Safety Services override signals.

FR3.3: Aggregate and expose cycle-phase performance metrics (actual vs. budgeted phase durations, load weight, travel speed, dump duration) via standardized Telemetry interface to enable verification of p95 cycle time against CT_target and detection of budget deviations.

FR3.4: Accept parameterized subsystem performance profiles and cycle task definitions via Tasking interface, and trigger offline duty-cycle characterization to update phase-time budgets without requiring code deployment.

revise.why: The current DP3+DP4 statement is correct but abstract. Revise to explicitly decompose into four sub-DPs corresponding to the next-level sub-FRs: (DP3.1) Parameterized duty-cycle characterization model with offline budget computation engine; (DP3.2) Real-time phase-gating state machine with safety-override arbitration; (DP3.3) Telemetry aggregation and export layer with fallback behavior for missing metrics; (DP3.4) Tasking interface handler for profile updates and re-characterization triggers. This decomposition operationalizes the architectural recommendations and clarifies the internal module structure of NGHE Solution.

assumptions: Subsystem performance profiles (load duration, travel speed, dump duration) are stable and can be characterized offline; significant hardware wear or environmental drift is detected via telemetry and triggers re-characterization.
Vehicle subsystem can expose performance metrics (actual load, speed feedback) via Network Link with acceptable latency (< 1s) to enable real-time phase-gating feedback.
Safety Services can issue override signals to NGHE Solution via SafetyAlert interface with priority over phase-gating enforcement; NGHE Solution honors overrides immediately without delay.

Worksite safety plan and operational policies (e.g., maximum load weight, terrain conditions, cycle-time target CT_target) are provided as inputs to duty-cycle characterization and remain stable during a work session.
Phase-gating enforcement tolerance ε (acceptable deviation from budgeted phase duration) is derived from worksite safety plan and is achievable by Vehicle subsystem actuators.
open_risks: Risk: Duty-cycle characterization model may become stale if subsystem performance degrades due to wear or environmental changes (e.g., muddy terrain increases travel time). Mitigation: Implement periodic re-characterization triggered by telemetry anomalies (e.g., actual phase duration exceeds budgeted + ε for N consecutive cycles). Define re-characterization frequency and trigger thresholds in FR3.1 DP.

Risk: Phase-gating enforcement may conflict with real-time operational constraints not captured in offline budgets (e.g., unexpected obstacle during travel phase). Mitigation: Define fallback behavior in FR3.2 DP—if phase-gating detects budget overrun, NGHE Solution must emit SafetyAlert and allow Safety Services to override phase-gating. Ensure Vehicle can abort phase gracefully.

Risk: Telemetry latency or loss may cause NGHE Solution to make phase-gating decisions based on stale data. Mitigation: Define Telemetry contract in FR3.3 DP with acceptable latency bounds and fallback behavior (e.g., degrade to pre-computed budgets if telemetry unavailable for > 2s).

Risk: Safety Services override signals may arrive too late to prevent phase-time constraint violation. Mitigation: Ensure SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional and high-priority; define maximum latency for override signal propagation (e.g., < 100ms) in FR3.2 DP.

Risk: Parameterization of duty-cycle characterization model may be incomplete, missing subsystem-specific performance factors (e.g., load distribution, terrain slope). Mitigation: Conduct sensitivity analysis during FR3.1 design to identify critical parameters; define fallback characterization model for unknown parameters.
evidence_needed: Subsystem performance profile data: measured load duration, travel speed, dump duration under representative worksite conditions (terrain type, load weight range, ambient temperature). Validate that profiles are stable across multiple work sessions.

Vehicle Network Link interface specification: define which telemetry metrics Vehicle exposes (actual load, speed feedback, phase completion signals) and at what frequency. Measure latency and jitter of telemetry transmission.

Safety Services SafetyAlert interface specification: define override signal semantics, priority rules, and maximum latency for override propagation. Conduct safety analysis to verify that Safety Services can abort phase-gating before safety constraint violation occurs.

Worksite safety plan and operational policies: document cycle-time target CT_target, maximum load weight, terrain conditions, and acceptable phase-time tolerance ε. Validate that ε is achievable by Vehicle subsystem.

Acceptance test plan for FR3.2 (phase-gating enforcement): define test scenarios including nominal operation, budget overrun detection, Safety Services override, and telemetry loss. Measure actual phase durations and verify they remain within ±ε of budgeted durations.

Parameterization sensitivity analysis: identify which subsystem performance factors have the greatest impact on phase-time budgets. Determine which parameters must be measured vs. estimated, and define fallback values for unknown parameters.

Re-characterization trigger thresholds: define telemetry anomaly detection rules (e.g., actual phase duration exceeds budgeted + ε for N consecutive cycles) and re-characterization frequency. Validate that re-characterization can be completed within acceptable time window (e.g., < 5 minutes) without disrupting ongoing work.
